mnemonics.title: POINT OPERATOR MEMORY
mnemonics.content: P-O-I-N-T: Pixel Output Is a Nonlinear Transformation - wait, actually: Pixel Output Is Neighboring Transformation-independent. Point operators transform each pixel INDEPENDENTLY of its neighbors.

mnemonics.title: LINEAR FILTERING STEPS
mnemonics.content: S-L-I-D-E: Slide kernel, Lookup neighbors, Input weighted sum, Divide by kernel size, Extend to all pixels. Remember: Convolution slides, computes weighted sum at each position.

mnemonics.title: IMAGE PROCESSING vs COMPUTER VISION
mnemonics.content: IP PREPROCESSES, CV INTERPRETS. Image Processing PREPS the image, Computer Vision UNDERSTANDS the image.

mnemonics.title: DIGITAL CAMERA COMPONENTS
mnemonics.content: O-S-A-P-S: Optics, Sensor, ADC, Processor, Storage. Light enters through Optics, Sensor captures, ADC converts, Processor handles, Storage saves.

acronyms.usage: Converts analog signals from camera sensor to digital values for processing
Type of image sensor that converts light to electrical charges
Alternative sensor technology to CCD, more power efficient
Algorithm used to transform images to frequency domain for analysis
Color model used to represent colors in digital images
The extent of the scene captured by the camera

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a flashlight (kernel) sliding across a dark image (canvas). At each position, the flashlight illuminates and mixes colors from nearby pixels according to the filter weights, painting a new transformed image.
Think of each pixel as an individual employee receiving a personal raise or adjustment. What happens to one does not affect what happens to others - each pixel is processed independently.

Picture dropping a pebble in water - the ripples spread out smoothly in all directions. The Gaussian filter works similarly, spreading pixel values with the strongest influence at the center, fading outward like water ripples.

Edges are like coastlines on a map - where land meets water (light meets dark). Edge detection highlights these boundaries by finding where color changes rapidly, like drawing outlines in a coloring book.

analogies.analogy: Like developing a photograph in a darkroom: the camera captures light (acquisition), then various chemicals and techniques enhance the image (processing), and finally you get the final print (output). The raw capture needs processing to become useful.

Like blending a smoothie: the kernel is the recipe specifying how much of each fruit to add, and each blender position (pixel neighborhood) gets blended according to the recipe to produce a new smooth result.

analogies.analization: A kernel is like a stencil or template placed over the image. At each position, you look only at what shows through the stencil holes, apply the weights, and produce one output pixel - like using a cookie cutter to stamp shapes repeatedly.

analogies.analogy: Like listening to music: the original image is like hearing the full song (spatial), while the frequency domain is like seeing the individual instruments and notes separately (high frequencies = sharp notes/details, low frequencies = bass/dramatic areas).
